desc: LA's most spectacular insect. Metallic blue-black with orange wings. Hunts tarantulas — paralyzes them with one of the most painful stings in the insect world. Schmidt Pain Index 4/4.

fm.Color: Metallic blue-black body; vivid orange wings
fm.Sting: Schmidt Pain Index 4/4 — among most painful insect stings on Earth. Brief (3–5 min).
fm.Behavior: ♀ hunts tarantulas; paralyzes with sting; drags to burrow; lays egg on paralyzed spider

desc: Second species of tarantula hawk in LA. Similar to Pepsis but slightly smaller. Same orange wings, excruciating sting.

fm.Color: Blue-black body; orange wings (darker than Pepsis)
fm.Sting: Schmidt Pain Index 4/4 — comparable to Pepsis
fm.Behavior: Same tarantula-hunting behavior as Pepsis

desc: The common yellowjacket of LA picnics. Ground-nesting. Scavenges meat and sweets. Aggressive defender. Causes most wasp stings.

fm.Color: Yellow and black banded; bright pattern
fm.Sting: Painful; repeated stinging; allergic reactions possible
fm.Behavior: Ground nest; scavenger; aggressive near food; peak aggression fall
fm.Habitat: Underground nests in gardens, parks, natural areas

desc: The common golden paper wasp of LA eaves and porches. Builds open-comb umbrella nest. Less aggressive than yellowjackets.

fm.Sting: Painful but less aggressive than Vespula
fm.Behavior: Open paper nest under eaves; predator of caterpillars; social

desc: Introduced. Now the most abundant paper wasp in urban LA. More yellow than native P. aurifer.

fm.Color: Bright yellow and black; antennae orange-tipped
fm.Behavior: Nests in any cavity — walls, mailboxes, grills, patio umbrellas

desc: Black and yellow thread-waisted wasp that builds mud tube nests on walls. Stuffs paralyzed spiders inside for larvae.

fm.Color: Black and yellow; very thin waist
fm.Behavior: Builds mud tube nests; hunts spiders; stuffs into tubes; solitary
fm.Habitat: Buildings, bridges, walls, overhangs — any sheltered surface

desc: Metallic blue-black. Does NOT build own nest — repurposes Sceliphron nests. Specializes in hunting black widows.

fm.Behavior: Repurposes mud dauber nests; specializes in hunting black widows and other cobweb spiders
fm.Habitat: Near existing mud dauber nests; urban structures

desc: Slender black wasp. Builds parallel mud tubes resembling organ pipes.

fm.Behavior: Builds "organ pipe" mud tubes; fills with paralyzed spiders

desc: Large, spectacular orange-and-black wasp. Digs burrows in sandy soil. Provisions with paralyzed katydids/grasshoppers.

fm.Color: Head/thorax black with golden pubescence; abdomen orange
fm.Sting: Can sting but non-aggressive; focused on digging
fm.Behavior: Digs burrows; hunts katydids/grasshoppers; paralyzes and drags to burrow

desc: Massive wasp that hunts cicadas. ♀ catches cicada in flight, stings it, flies it to burrow. Impressive.

fm.Color: Black with yellow abdominal bands; amber wings
fm.Sting: Can sting but rarely does; focused on cicadas
fm.Behavior: ♀ hunts cicadas; catches in flight; burrows in soil
fm.Habitat: Sandy soil near areas with cicadas

desc: Not an ant — a wingless female wasp. Dense orange velvet pile. Parasitizes ground-nesting bee/wasp larvae. Painful sting.

fm.Color: ♀: wingless; black with dense orange-red velvet pile. ♂: winged; less colorful.
fm.Sting: ♀: very painful (Schmidt 3/4); "cow killer" nickname (can't actually kill cows)
fm.Behavior: ♀ enters host wasp/bee nests; lays eggs on host larvae
fm.Season: May–Oct; ♀ wander ground seeking host nests

desc: Extraordinary — dense white velvet pile makes it look like a thistledown seed blowing across desert sand. Unique camouflage.

fm.Color: ♀: entirely white/cream dense velvet pile; resembles thistledown
fm.Behavior: ♀ walks on hot desert sand; white pile reflects heat; parasitizes bees
fm.Habitat: Desert scrub, sandy washes — NE LA County

desc: Stocky wasp that burrows in sand. Provisions nest with paralyzed flies. Rapid circular flights around nesting area.

fm.Behavior: Mass nesting in sand; provisions with flies; rapid buzzy flight
fm.Habitat: Sandy beaches, sandy soil, sand dunes

desc: Not a true hornet. Black with white face markings. Builds large paper nest in trees. Aggressive defender.

fm.Color: Black with white face and body markings
fm.Sting: Aggressive near nest; painful; repeated stinging
fm.Behavior: Large enclosed paper nest in trees/structures; social
fm.Habitat: Trees, shrubs, building overhangs — montane areas more common

desc: True hornet — the only Vespa in western NA. Large. Brown/yellow. Rare in LA Co.

fm.Behavior: Paper nest in tree cavities; hunts other insects

desc: Tiny wasp in obligate mutualism with figs. Pollinates fig flowers internally. Every fig you eat was pollinated by a fig wasp.

fm.Behavior: ♀ enters fig through ostiole; pollinates; lays eggs; dies inside fig

desc: Tiny parasitoid wasp. Larvae develop inside hornworm caterpillars, emerging as white cocoon clusters on caterpillar's back.

fm.Behavior: ♀ lays eggs inside caterpillar; larvae feed internally; emerge as white cocoons on host back

desc: Metallic blue-black wasp that hunts crickets. Drags paralyzed cricket to burrow. Spectacular blue color.

fm.Sting: Can sting but focused on crickets
fm.Behavior: Hunts crickets/katydids; drags to burrow in soil

desc: Stocky black-and-yellow wasp. Hunts stink bugs. Ground nester in sandy soil.

fm.Behavior: Hunts stink bugs; ground nester

desc: Dark paper wasp with red markings. Builds umbrella nests under protected overhangs.

fm.Behavior: Social; paper nest under overhangs

desc: Large, clubbed-antenna sawfly (not a true wasp). Larvae on willows/elms. Impressive size.

fm.Color: Dark metallic blue-black; yellow lateral spots
fm.Behavior: Adults on flowers; larvae on willow/elm leaves

desc: Unmistakable — ♀ has extraordinarily elongated abdomen (2 in+). Parasitoid of June beetle grubs.

fm.Size: ♀: up to 3 in total (extremely elongated abdomen)
fm.Behavior: ♀ probes soil with long abdomen to reach beetle grubs; parasitoid

desc: Builds paper nest in trees (not underground like V. pensylvanica).

fm.Color: Black with yellow markings (not white like D. maculata)
fm.Behavior: Aerial paper nest in trees; social

desc: Small wasp with flag-like abdomen held high. Parasitoid of cockroach egg cases. Beneficial indoors.

fm.Color: Black; small; abdomen on high petiole like a flag
fm.Behavior: Seeks cockroach egg cases; lays egg inside; larva destroys eggs; beneficial
fm.Habitat: Urban, synanthropic — wherever cockroaches are found

desc: Large black mason wasp with white abdominal band. Nests in pre-existing cavities. Hunts caterpillars.

fm.Color: Black with single white band on first abdominal segment
fm.Behavior: Nests in beetle borings, hollow stems, bee hotels; hunts caterpillars

desc: Introduced. Often nests in wall voids and structures. More aggressive than Western YJ around food sources.

fm.Color: Yellow and black; very similar to V. pensylvanica but thicker face marks
fm.Behavior: Nests in wall voids, attics, underground; scavenges extensively

desc: Slender, long-legged paper wasp. Builds small pendant nests. Less aggressive than Polistes.

fm.Color: Dark brown with yellow markings; very long legs
fm.Behavior: Small pendant paper nest under eaves; long dangling legs in flight

desc: Dark wasp with golden spot on thorax. Hunts caterpillars in chaparral.

fm.Color: Black with golden dorsal thorax spot
fm.Sting: Can sting but focused on caterpillars
fm.Behavior: Hunts caterpillars; carries them in flight to burrow

desc: Tiny wasp that induces spectacular "oak apple" galls on oak trees — golf-ball sized or larger. Complex lifecycle.

fm.Behavior: ♀ injects egg + chemicals into oak bud; tree grows gall around larva; alternating sexual/asexual generations
fm.Season: Galls visible year-round; adults emerge spring

desc: Most abundant paper wasp after P. dominula.

fm.Behavior: Open paper nest under eaves

desc: Large hairy wasp. Parasitizes white grubs. Beneficial.

fm.Color: Black with yellow abdominal spots; hairy
fm.Behavior: Digs to parasitize scarab grubs; visits flowers

desc: Orange-red velvet ant. ♀ wingless. Painful sting.

fm.Behavior: ♀ walks ground seeking host nests

desc: Invasive. Supercolonies. Dominant urban ant.

fm.Behavior: Trails; tends aphids; displaces natives

desc: Velvety tree ant — large conspicuous ant nesting in tree cavities and under bark. Trails on tree trunks. Emits strong acrid coconut-like odor when disturbed. Native.

fm.Color: Dark brown-black; acrid coconut odor when disturbed

desc: California harvester ant — large red ant building crater-shaped nests with cleared vegetation disc. Extremely painful sting — among the most painful of any ant. Seed harvester.

fm.Color: Red; crater nest with cleared disc

desc: Southern fire ant — native fire ant. Stings painfully in swarming defense. Nests in soil mounds. Less aggressive than imported red fire ant but still formidable.
Dark rover ant — tiny invasive ant from South America. Invades structures in massive numbers. Attracted to sweet liquids. Can be extremely abundant. Difficult to control.
Hidalgo mason wasp — small solitary wasp nesting in pre-existing holes including bee hotels. Provisions with moth larvae. Black with pale markings. Garden beneficial.

fm.Color: Black with pale bands; uses bee hotels

desc: Paper wasp — small docile paper wasp building small open-comb nests. Less aggressive than P. dominula. Important caterpillar predator. Beneficial garden wasp.
Milde's tarantula hawk — large dark spider wasp with orange wings. Hunts tarantulas. Famously painful sting (Schmidt pain scale 4/4). Third Pepsis species in the LA basin.

fm.Warning: Among the most painful stings of any insect

desc: Coconut smell when crushed. Kitchen pest.

fm.Behavior: Kitchen trails; coconut odor

desc: Apache paper wasp — native paper wasp of arid habitats. Smaller and more slender than P. dominula. Builds open paper nests under eaves and in shrubs.
Thread-waisted sand wasp — large metallic blue-black wasp hunting katydids. Digs burrow in sandy soil, provisions with paralyzed katydids. Desert and arid scrub.
Meadow mason wasp — small solitary vespid nesting in pre-existing holes. Provisions with caterpillars. Common in gardens.
California velvet ant — wingless female wasp covered in dense orange-red velvet pile. Males winged. Extremely painful sting — 'cow killer.' Parasitizes ground-nesting bees.
Elegant grass-carrier wasp — slender black wasp that stuffs grass and plant material into cavities for nesting. Provisions with tree crickets. Common in bee hotels.

fm.Color: Black; stuffs grass into nest cavities

desc: Cross-marked potter wasp — builds beautiful miniature clay pot nests attached to twigs. Each pot provisioned with paralyzed caterpillars. Remarkable mud architecture.

fm.Behavior: Builds exquisite mud pot nests on twigs

desc: Boll's potter wasp — builds remarkable mud pot nests attached to twigs. Each pot provisioned with paralyzed caterpillars. Master potter.
California yellowjacket — endemic California yellowjacket. Less aggressive than German yellowjacket. Nests in ground cavities. Important predator of pest caterpillars.
Red imported fire ant — highly invasive ant with extremely painful sting. Originally from South America. Massive colonies. Spreading into SoCal. Medical and agricultural threat.

fm.Warning: Highly invasive — very painful sting — spreading in SoCal

desc: Hump-backed beewolf — medium wasp with humped thorax. Hunts small bees. Provisions underground burrow. Colonial nester.

fm.Color: Black and yellow; humped; hunts bees

desc: Hairy-footed scoliid — large hairy wasp hunting scarab beetle grubs underground. Important biocontrol of white grubs. Visits flowers.
Dark paper wasp with variable facial patterns. Recognizes nestmate faces individually — advanced social cognition.

fm.Color: Dark brown; variable facial markings (individual recognition)
fm.Behavior: Social; facial recognition of nestmates (rare in insects)

desc: Metallic blue spider wasp that builds mud cells in cavities. Hunts spiders, stuffs them in mud partitions.

fm.Behavior: Builds mud cells in cavities; hunts spiders; common in bee hotels
fm.Habitat: Cavities — bee hotels, hollow stems, walls

desc: Large, entirely black digger wasp. Hunts katydids and grasshoppers. Impressive size and digging power.

fm.Behavior: Digs deep burrows; provisions with paralyzed Orthoptera

desc: Western subspecies of common mud dauber with more yellow marking. Builds characteristic mud tubes.

fm.Color: Black with yellow legs and markings
fm.Behavior: Builds mud tubes on walls; provisions with spiders

desc: Large black-and-orange velvet ant with extraordinarily painful sting. ♀ wingless.

fm.Color: Black with orange-red velvet pile; robust
fm.Behavior: ♀ walks ground seeking host nests; ♂ winged, smaller

desc: Only true hornet in NA (not the Asian giant hornet). Brown and yellow. Much larger than yellowjackets.

fm.Color: Brown and yellow; much larger than yellowjackets
fm.Behavior: Paper nest in tree hollows; hunts other insects; attracted to lights at night

desc: Small, dark potter wasp that nests in small holes and keyholes. Has caused aviation incidents by blocking pitot tubes.

fm.Behavior: Nests in small cavities including keyholes, instrument ports

desc: Ground-nesting wasp that hunts weevils and small beetles. Yellow and black. Valuable biocontrol.

fm.Behavior: Hunts weevils and small beetles; ground nester in hard-packed soil

desc: Native golden fire ant. Smaller colonies than imported fire ant. Declining as Argentine ant displaces it.

fm.Behavior: Stings in defense; declining from Argentine ant competition

desc: Native desert ant with distinctive crater-shaped nest entrance. Bicolored — red head, dark gaster. Fast runner.

fm.Colony: Crater-shaped nest entrance in sandy soil (diagnostic)
fm.Behavior: Extremely fast runner; forages at high temperatures
fm.Habitat: Desert, sandy soil, hot open areas

desc: Large native carpenter ant. Bicolored. Nests in dead wood but does NOT eat wood (unlike termites). Nocturnal forager.

fm.Colony: In dead wood, tree cavities; does not eat wood
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal foraging trails; large workers have powerful mandibles
fm.Habitat: Oak woodland, urban with dead wood

desc: Female has ovipositor longer than body — drills through wood to parasitize horntail larvae.

fm.Description: Female has ovipositor longer than body — drills through wood to parasitize horntail larvae

desc: Black wasp with blue wings and orange abdominal spots. Parasitizes Japanese beetle grubs.

fm.Description: Black wasp with blue wings and orange abdominal spots

desc: Large wood wasp (sawfly). Female drills into hardwoods to lay eggs. Hosts giant ichneumons.
Brown paper wasp. Builds small open-face nests under eaves. Less aggressive than yellowjackets.
Close relative of L. occidentale. Found in mountain areas on oaks and conifers.

fm.Habitat: Found in mountain areas on oaks and conifers

desc: Burrowing wasp of sandy beaches and washes. Provisions nest with flies caught in flight.

fm.Habitat: Burrowing wasp of sandy beaches and washes

desc: Supercolonial ant forming vast networks. Tends honeydew-producing insects. Spreading through urban areas.
Red ant building gravel mound nests in desert and grassland. Painful sting. Important seed disperser.

fm.Sting: Very painful — rated 3 on Schmidt scale

desc: Western mound-building ant. Large thatch mounds in mountain meadows. Sprays formic acid.
Slender wasp hunting caterpillars. Digs burrow, stings and buries prey for larva. Uses pebble tool to tamp soil.

fm.Prey: Caterpillars — paralyzed and buried for larvae
fm.Behavior: Uses pebble as tool to tamp soil over burrow

desc: Metallic blue spider wasp hunting wolf spiders. Paralyzes and buries prey in burrow.
Brilliant metallic green-blue wasp. Cleptoparasite of mud dauber nests. Rolls into ball when attacked.
Black wasp parasitizing white grubs (scarab larvae) underground. Important biocontrol of lawn grubs.

fm.Nesting: Digs to parasitize white grubs underground
fm.Prey: Scarab larvae (Japanese beetle, June beetle grubs)

desc: Large hairy wasp digging into soil to parasitize scarab grubs. Yellow abdominal bands.

fm.Color: Black with yellow abdominal bands; hairy
fm.Nesting: Digs into soil for scarab grubs

desc: Tiny metallic green wasp. Parasitoid of gall-forming insects. Extremely diverse family.

fm.Role: Parasitoid of gall-forming insects on oaks

desc: Tiny fast-running ant with erratic movement. Indoor nuisance pest. Multiple queens per colony.

fm.Behavior: Erratic zigzag movement — "crazy ant"

desc: Small ant raising heart-shaped abdomen over body when alarmed. Tree-dwelling. Nests in dead wood.

fm.Defense: Raises abdomen over body when alarmed

desc: LA's most common structural termite. Winged reproductives swarm Sep–Nov. Does not need soil contact.

fm.Damage: Eats wood; distinctive hexagonal fecal pellets

desc: Native subterranean termite. Builds mud tubes from soil to wood. Much larger colonies than drywood.

fm.Damage: Mud shelter tubes diagnostic; requires soil moisture

desc: Small dark ant nesting under sidewalks and pavement cracks. Characteristic sand mounds at pavement joints. Aggressive toward other ant colonies — sidewalk 'ant wars' in spring.

fm.Color: Dark brown-black; grooved head and thorax
fm.Habitat: Under pavement, sidewalk cracks — sand mounds at joints
fm.Behavior: Mass 'ant wars' between colonies on warm spring days

desc: Invasive tramp ant with dimorphic workers — major workers have enormous disproportionate heads. Displaces native ants. One of the world's 100 worst invasive species (IUCN).

fm.Habitat: Disturbed urban areas — soil, leaf litter
fm.Status: IUCN 100 worst invasive species; displaces native ants

desc: Spectacular parasitoid wasp — female's ovipositor can exceed 4 inches, used to drill through wood and parasitize wood-boring beetle larvae. Completely harmless to humans.

fm.Habitat: Near dead/dying trees — drills into wood to parasitize beetle larvae
fm.Note: Cannot sting humans despite fearsome appearance

desc: Tiny dark ant that runs in erratic, jerky patterns (hence 'crazy'). Extremely long antennae and legs. Invasive pantropical tramp ant. Forages in kitchens.

fm.Color: Dark brown-black; very long antennae and legs
fm.Behavior: Erratic, jerky running pattern diagnostic

desc: Tiny jet-black ant. One of the most common indoor-invading ants in urban LA. Trails along edges. Slow-moving. Nests in soil, wall cracks, under objects.

fm.Habitat: Indoors — trails along baseboards; nests in wall cracks

desc: Tiny parasitoid wasp. Larvae develop inside cabbage white caterpillars, then emerge and spin white silk cocoon clusters on or near the dead host. Important biocontrol.

fm.Behavior: White cocoon clusters on dead caterpillars diagnostic

desc: Microscopic parasitoid wasp commercially sold for biocontrol. Female lays egg inside living aphid — aphid swells into brown 'mummy.' Foundational biological control agent.
Tiny metallic green parasitoid wasp attacking blow fly pupae. Model organism for genetics research. Important natural enemy of filth flies.

fm.Habitat: Near fly breeding sites — parasitizes blow fly pupae
fm.Role: Natural enemy of blow flies; genetics model organism

desc: Small dark brown ant with painful sting. Invasive from East Asia. Displaces native ants. Nests in leaf litter and rotting wood. Spreading in urban areas.

fm.Habitat: Leaf litter, rotting wood — urban parks

desc: Brilliant red-orange velvet ant. Wingless ♀ with extremely painful sting ('cow killer'). ♂ winged, black.
Small dark ant active in WINTER when other ants are dormant. Workers have distensible gasters for storing honeydew. Unique cold-weather ecology.
Tiny yellowish ant. Lives by stealing food from other ant colonies through tiny tunnels. Too small for host ants to pursue. Also pantry pest.
Tiny ant — dark head, pale body gives ghostly appearance. Indoor trailing ant in kitchens. Tropical invasive.

fm.Color: Dark head; pale translucent body — ghost-like

desc: Tiny dark ant with pale tarsi. Forms enormous supercolonies. Major nuisance ant in SoCal — difficult to control.
Large orange-and-black ichneumon wasp. Parasitoid of moth caterpillars. Long ovipositor. Harmless. Common at lights.
Large golden-orange ichneumon attracted to lights at night. Parasitizes caterpillars. Slender with compressed abdomen.
One of the world's smallest insects — 0.5mm. Parasitoid of insect eggs. Wings are feather-like. Invisible to naked eye but ecologically important.
Large rusty-orange spider wasp. Hunts large spiders (wolf spiders, trapdoor spiders). Dramatic prey-hunting behavior.
Live oak apple gall wasp — tiny wasp inducing large round galls on coast live oak leaves. Galls up to 2 inches, green turning brown. Very common — look for round balls on oak leaves.

fm.Color: Tiny; gall is conspicuous round ball on oak leaf

desc: Pumpkin gall wasp — tiny cynipid inducing round galls on oak twigs resembling miniature pumpkins. Very common on scrub oaks. Alternating sexual and asexual generations.

fm.Color: Tiny; gall is ridged round ball on oak twig

desc: Beaked twig gall wasp — induces spindle-shaped woody galls on oak twigs. Common on coast live oak throughout LA foothill canyons.

fm.Color: Tiny; gall is spindle/beak-shaped on oak twig

desc: Willow apple gall sawfly — not a true wasp but a sawfly. Induces round reddish galls on willow leaves resembling small apples. Common along riparian corridors.

fm.Color: Black; gall is round and reddish on willow leaf

desc: Pergande's harvester ant — large black desert ant forming long foraging trails. Harvests seeds and stores them in deep nests. Important desert ecosystem engineer. Trails can stretch 100+ feet.

fm.Behavior: Long foraging trails; harvests seeds

desc: Irregular spindle gall wasp — tiny cynipid inducing elongated spindle-shaped woody galls on coast live oak twigs. Very common in foothill oak woodland. Complex alternating life cycle.

fm.Color: Tiny; gall is woody spindle on oak twig

desc: King's gall wasp — tiny cynipid inducing small round detachable galls on live oak leaves. Galls fall to ground and wasp emerges from soil. Common.

fm.Color: Tiny; gall is small round ball on oak leaf

desc: Mason wasp — solitary black-and-yellow vespid nesting in pre-existing holes. Provisions nest with caterpillars. Common on buildings and fences.
Douglas's gall wasp — tiny cynipid inducing leaf galls on coast live oak. Alternating sexual and asexual generations produce different gall types on the same tree.

fm.Color: Tiny; leaf galls on live oak

desc: Rough harvester ant — large red-brown ant building conspicuous crater nests in open desert and grassland. Extremely painful sting. Long-lived colonies. Important seed disperser.

fm.Warning: Very painful sting — among the most painful of any ant

desc: Andre's harvester ant — black harvester ant common in chaparral and oak woodland. Forms long foraging columns. Nests in soil with inconspicuous entrance. Collects seeds.

fm.Behavior: Long foraging columns collecting seeds

desc: Shield gall wasp — tiny cynipid inducing small flat shield-shaped galls on coast live oak leaves. One of many cynipid wasps exploiting California oaks.

fm.Color: Tiny; flat disc-shaped gall on oak leaf

desc: Crystal gall wasp — induces translucent crystalline galls on oak leaves. Among the most delicate and beautiful of all gall forms.

fm.Color: Tiny; translucent crystal-like gall on oak leaf

desc: Bakker's gall wasp — another oak gall cynipid producing small round galls. Part of the extraordinarily diverse community of gall wasps on California oaks.

fm.Color: Tiny; small round galls on oak

desc: Uniform velvet ant — wingless female wasp (not an ant) covered in pale golden-brown velvet pile. Parasitizes ground-nesting bee and wasp nests. Painful sting.
Eucalyptus gall wasp — tiny chalcid wasp inducing blister galls on eucalyptus leaves. Invasive from Australia. Very common on blue gum eucalyptus.

fm.Color: Tiny; blister galls on eucalyptus leaves

desc: Fullaway's gall wasp — tiny cynipid inducing stem galls on coast live oak. Part of the diverse community of gall wasps on California oaks.

fm.Color: Tiny; stem galls on live oak

desc: Live oak woolly gall wasp — induces conspicuous woolly galls on coast live oak twigs. White fuzzy galls are very noticeable. Common throughout LA foothill canyons.

fm.Color: Tiny; woolly white galls on oak twigs

desc: Navigator big-headed ant — dimorphic ant with major workers ('soldiers') having disproportionately huge heads. Minor workers forage; majors defend and crack seeds.

fm.Size: ⅛ in; major workers have huge heads
fm.Color: Brown; major workers with enormous head

desc: Woolly leaf gall wasp — induces soft woolly galls on live oak leaves. Part of the rich cynipid community on California oaks.

fm.Color: Tiny; woolly galls on oak leaves

desc: Sutton's gall wasp — tiny cynipid inducing round detachable galls on coast live oak leaves. Galls fall to ground where wasp develops. Very common.

fm.Color: Tiny; round detachable galls on oak leaves

desc: Clear-thoraxed carpenter ant — large carpenter ant with partly translucent thorax. Nests in dead wood. Common in mountain oak woodland.
Clustered gall wasp — induces clustered round galls on oak twigs. Galls form tight clusters. Very common on scrub oaks in chaparral.

fm.Color: Tiny; clustered round galls on oak twigs

desc: Saw-horned gall wasp — tiny cynipid inducing stem galls on oaks. Named for serrated antennae. Complex alternating-generation life cycle.
Moki field ant — medium-sized brown ant building thatched mound nests. Common in chaparral and open woodland. Tends aphids for honeydew. Aggressive defenders.
Knobby-headed mason wasp — solitary vespid nesting in pre-existing holes. Provisions with caterpillars. Black with pale markings. Common on buildings.
Francoeur's field ant — medium brown-black ant of chaparral and woodland. Builds soil mound nests. Tends aphids for honeydew. Aggressive nest defense — sprays formic acid.

fm.Behavior: Sprays formic acid in defense

desc: Baker's carpenter ant — large bicolored carpenter ant. Red-brown thorax with dark head and gaster. Nests in dead wood in mountain oak woodland.
Shiny harvester ant — medium red-brown ant with somewhat shiny integument. Builds crater nests in open areas. Painful sting. Seed harvester.
Crazy pyramid ant — fast-moving ant with distinctive pyramid-shaped bump on thorax. Builds volcano-shaped soil nests. Very common in open sunny areas. Named for erratic running.

fm.Color: Dark brown; pyramid bump on thorax
fm.Behavior: Extremely fast; erratic running pattern

desc: Ficus gall wasp — tiny agaonid wasp inducing galls on ornamental ficus leaves. Introduced from Asia. Very common on Ficus microcarpa in LA. Causes leaf distortion.

fm.Color: Tiny; leaf galls on Ficus trees

desc: Hyatt's big-headed ant — dimorphic ant with major workers having enormously enlarged heads for seed-cracking. Common in desert scrub and chaparral. Nests in soil.

fm.Size: ⅛ in; majors have huge heads
fm.Color: Brown; major workers with disproportionate head

desc: Honeydew gall wasp — cynipid inducing round stem galls on oak that exude sticky honeydew. Attracts ants, wasps, and butterflies. Very conspicuous.

fm.Color: Tiny; round stem galls that ooze honeydew

desc: Intermediate oak disc wasp — tiny gall wasp inducing flat disc-shaped galls on oak leaves. Very common on coast live oak throughout LA foothills.

fm.Color: Tiny; flat disc galls on oak leaves

desc: Provancher's sand wasp — large sand wasp nesting in soil. Provisions nest with weevils. Colonial nesting aggregations in sandy areas.

fm.Color: Black and yellow; nests in sandy soil

desc: Six-banded sand wasp — medium crabronid with yellow bands. Hunts small beetles to provision nest. Colonial. Common in open sandy areas.

fm.Color: Black with yellow bands; hunts beetles

desc: Saucer gall wasp — induces large flat saucer-shaped galls on oak leaves. Among the largest leaf galls in the oak gall wasp community. Very conspicuous.

fm.Color: Tiny; large flat saucer galls on oak

desc: Apache twig ant — slender elongated ant nesting inside hollow twigs and acacia thorns. Large eyes for an ant. Very fast. Painful sting relative to size.
Urn gall wasp — induces distinctive urn-shaped or cup-shaped galls on oak twigs. Detachable when mature. Part of the rich California oak gall fauna.

fm.Color: Tiny; urn-shaped galls on oak twigs

desc: Dusty mason wasp — small dark solitary wasp nesting in pre-existing holes. Provisions with caterpillars. Common on buildings and fences.

fm.Color: Dark with pale markings; dusty appearance

desc: Convergent sand wasp — medium wasp provisioning nest with beetles. Important for scientific beetle surveys — researchers check wasp prey for rare beetle species.
Bicolored carpenter ant — large ant with red thorax and black head/gaster. Common in mountain oak woodland. Excavates galleries in dead wood. Nocturnal forager.
Ruptured twig gall wasp — induces irregular ruptured swellings on oak twigs. Galls split open when wasp emerges. Common on coast live oak.

fm.Color: Tiny; ruptured twig swellings on oak

desc: Edwards' pollen wasp — unusual wasp that feeds larvae POLLEN (not insects). Visits penstemons. One of the few vegetarian wasps. California native.

fm.Note: Feeds larvae pollen — unusual for wasps

desc: Moorish sneaking ant — tiny introduced ant. Males are wingless fighters that kill rival males in nest. Unusual life history. Urban areas.
Wheeler's honeypot ant — desert ant with replete workers ('honeypots') that store liquid food in enormously distended abdomens. Hang from nest ceiling. Remarkable biology.

fm.Behavior: Living food storage — swollen workers hang from ceiling

desc: Many-spotted beewolf — medium wasp that hunts bees to provision nest. Digs burrow in sandy soil. Paralyzes bee and carries it to nest for larvae.

fm.Color: Black with yellow spots; hunts bees

desc: Eucalyptus stem gall wasp — tiny chalcid inducing swollen galls on eucalyptus leaf midribs and stems. Invasive from Australia. Seriously damages young eucalyptus.

fm.Color: Tiny; swollen galls on eucalyptus stems

desc: Purged ichneumon — large pale ichneumon wasp. Nocturnal parasitoid of caterpillars. Attracted to lights. Long ovipositor injects egg into host. Important biocontrol.

fm.Color: Pale orange-brown; long ovipositor; at lights

desc: Coquillett's pollen wasp — another pollen-feeding wasp visiting native flowers. Related to P. edwardsii. Provisions nest with pollen balls.
Irregular Spindle Gall Wasp. Gall wasp. Induces galls on oaks.
Beaked Spindle Gall Wasp. Gall wasp. Induces galls on oaks.
Dark-winged sand wasp — medium stocky wasp nesting in sandy soil. Hunts stink bugs to provision nest. Colonial nesting aggregations.

fm.Color: Black and yellow; dark wings; hunts stink bugs

desc: Cousin funnel ant — medium brown ant building distinctive crater-shaped nest entrances. Common in foothill chaparral. Important seed disperser. Active forager.
Bicolored pennant ant — small ant with distinctive bicolored gaster. Pantropical introduction. Nests in soil and structures. Spreading in urban SoCal.
White-banded spider wasp — black wasp with white abdominal bands. Hunts orb-weaving spiders. Drags paralyzed spider to burrow.

fm.Color: Black with white bands; hunts spiders

desc: Jumping Gall Wasp. Gall wasp. Induces galls on oaks.
Clustered Gall Wasp. Gall wasp. Induces galls on oaks.
Bowl Gall Wasp. Gall wasp. Induces galls on oaks.
Spiny Leaf Gall Wasp. Gall wasp. Induces galls on oaks.
Common Hover Fly Parasitoid Wasp. Ichneumon wasp. Parasitoid.
Bustamente mason wasp — solitary vespid nesting in pre-existing holes. Provisions with caterpillars. Common on buildings and wooden structures.
Washington's gall wasp — tiny cynipid inducing galls on oak twigs and leaves. Part of the remarkably diverse community of gall wasps on California oaks.
Abert's Thread-waisted Wasp. Digger wasp.
San Clemente Island Big-headed Ant. Ant.
Western Red-tailed Spider Wasp. Spider wasp.
Kin leucospid wasp — small parasitoid wasp with distinctive hump-backed shape and banded pattern. Ovipositor curves over back. Parasitizes solitary bee larvae in nests.

fm.Color: Black and yellow; curved ovipositor over back

desc: Fragile gall wasp — tiny cynipid inducing delicate galls on oak leaves. Complex alternating-generation life cycle producing different gall types in different seasons.

fm.Color: Tiny; delicate leaf galls on oak

desc: Smooth carpenter ant — large shiny carpenter ant with unusually smooth (hairless) integument. Nests in dead wood. Mountain and foothill oak woodland.
Stellar Gall Wasp. Gall wasp. Induces galls on oaks.
Honey pot ant — workers ('repletes') hang from nest ceiling with abdomens swollen to grape-size with stored honeydew. Living food storage. Desert species. Remarkable biology.

fm.Behavior: Living 'honey pots' store food in distended abdomens

desc: Parker's Thread-waisted Wasp. Digger wasp.
Philadelphia grass-carrier wasp — slender black wasp that provisions nest with grass and tree cricket prey. Nests in hollow tubes. Stuffs grass as nest partition.
Yellow gall wasp — tiny cynipid inducing galls on oak. Named for yellowish coloring. Part of the enormous diversity of oak-associated gall wasps in California.
Chaparral carpenter ant — medium carpenter ant of chaparral and scrub habitats. Nests in dead wood and plant stems. Nocturnal.
Worker-queen ant — tiny ant whose queens resemble workers (ergatoid). Nests under rocks in desert scrub. Unusual reproductive biology.
Dried Peach Gall Wasp. Gall wasp. Induces galls on oaks.
Scarlet velvet ant — brilliantly red-haired wingless female wasp ('velvet ant'). Parasitizes ground-nesting bee and wasp nests. Males winged but rarely seen. Extremely painful sting.
Bramble mason wasp — small dark solitary wasp nesting in hollow bramble stems and beetle holes. Provisions with caterpillars. Common in bee hotels. Beneficial.

fm.Color: Black with pale bands; in bramble stems

desc: Chrysothemis tarantula hawk — large spider wasp with dark body and orange-tinted wings. Hunts tarantulas. One of several Pepsis species in LA County mountains.
Ringed mason wasp — solitary wasp nesting in beetle holes and hollow stems. Provisions with caterpillars. Part of the diverse eumenine wasp community.

fm.Color: Black with yellow bands; ringed pattern

desc: Smoothest carpenter ant — large carpenter ant with extremely smooth shiny integument and sparse hair. In dead wood in mountain habitats. Related to C. laevigatus.
Crowned spider wasp — medium pompilid hunting spiders. Drags paralyzed spider to burrow for larvae. Black with distinctive pale head markings.

fm.Color: Black with pale head marks; hunts spiders

desc: Flat spider wasp — dark pompilid hunting ground spiders. Flattened body allows it to enter spider burrows. Drags paralyzed spider to own nest.
Club Gall Wasp. Gall wasp. Induces galls on oaks.
Hooked sand wasp — medium crabronid nesting in sandy soil. Provisions nest with various insects. Part of the diverse sand wasp community.

fm.Color: Black and yellow; in sandy soil

desc: Mottled Acorn Gall Wasp. Gall wasp. Induces galls on oaks.
Nevada sand wasp — medium sand wasp of arid habitats. Nests in sandy soil. Provisions with insects. Desert and foothill species.
Spectacular tapered gall wasp — induces elongated tapered stem galls on oak. Among the more distinctive gall forms. Foothill oak woodland.

fm.Color: Tiny; elongated tapered galls on oak stems

desc: Beautiful-winged spider wasp — small pompilid with iridescent wings. Hunts spiders. Uses pre-existing cavities for nesting rather than digging burrows.
Charcoal velvet ant — dark-haired wingless female wasp. Parasitizes ground-nesting bee nests. Runs rapidly over ground searching for host nests. Very painful sting.
Bullet-Gall Wasp. Gall wasp. Induces galls on oaks.
Western sycamore sawfly — small sawfly (not a true wasp) whose larvae skeletonize sycamore leaves. Larvae are caterpillar-like. Can defoliate sycamores. Native.
Live Oak Petiole Gall Wasp. Gall wasp. Induces plant galls, especially on oaks. Tiny.
Scoliid wasp. Parasitoid of scarab grubs. Large.
Gall wasp. Induces plant galls, especially on oaks. Tiny.
Sausage Flower Gall Wasp. Gall wasp. Induces plant galls, especially on oaks. Tiny.
Ant. Social colonies. Diverse ecological roles.
Digger wasp. Hunts insects. Nests in soil or wood.
Gall wasp. Induces plant galls, especially on oaks. Tiny.
Yogi Carpenter Ant. Ant. Social colonies. Diverse ecological roles.
Digger wasp. Hunts insects. Nests in soil or wood.
Encyrtid wasp. Tiny parasitoid of scale insects.
Spider wasp. Hunts spiders for larvae. Often large.
Wheeler's pollen wasp — unusual wasp feeding larvae on pollen instead of insects. On penstemon flowers. One of the few vegetarian wasp genera.
Gall wasp. Induces plant galls, especially on oaks. Tiny.
Blue Gum Gall Wasp. Eulophid wasp. Tiny parasitoid. Important biocontrol.
Bristly rose sawfly — small sawfly whose larvae feed gregariously on rose leaves. Can defoliate garden roses. Larvae look like tiny green caterpillars.

fm.Color: Black and yellow; larvae on roses

desc: High Noon Ant. Ant. Social colonies. Diverse ecological roles.
Ant. Social colonies. Diverse ecological roles.
Line-bellied mason wasp — small black solitary wasp with pale abdominal lines. Nests in pre-existing holes. Provisions with caterpillars.
Ball-tipped Gall Wasp. Gall wasp. Induces plant galls, especially on oaks. Tiny.
Purslane sawfly — tiny sawfly whose larvae feed on purslane. One of the few sawflies that feeds on succulent plants. Can help control weedy purslane.
Velvet ant. Wingless female. Very painful sting.
Gall wasp. Induces plant galls, especially on oaks. Tiny.
Gall wasp. Induces plant galls, especially on oaks. Tiny.
Split Twig Gall Wasp. Gall wasp. Induces plant galls, especially on oaks. Tiny.
Essig's Carpenter Ant. Ant. Social colonies. Diverse ecological roles.
California Big-headed Ant. Ant. Social colonies. Diverse ecological roles.
Ladybird Parasitoid Wasp. Braconid wasp. Parasitoid of caterpillars and aphids.
California mason wasp — small solitary wasp nesting in nail holes and crevices. Provisions with moth caterpillars. Common on buildings.
Vertical potter wasp — builds remarkable clay pot nests attached to twigs. Each pot provisioned with paralyzed caterpillars. Mud-daubing master architect.

fm.Behavior: Builds clay pot nests on twigs — remarkable architecture

desc: Round Honeydew Gall Wasp. Gall wasp. Induces plant galls, especially on oaks. Tiny.
Froggatt's fig wasp — tiny wasp that pollinates specific fig species. Obligate mutualism — fig cannot reproduce without wasp and vice versa. Coevolved for millions of years.
Peppertree Seed Chalcid. Torymid wasp. Parasitoid in galls and seeds.
Cuckoo wasp. Metallic jewel-like. Parasitizes other wasps.
leafy oak gall wasp. Gall wasp. Induces plant galls, especially on oaks. Tiny.
Fimbriate Gall Wasp. Gall wasp. Induces plant galls, especially on oaks. Tiny.
Two-spotted cuckoo wasp — small metallic green chrysidid. Cleptoparasite of solitary wasp nests. Brilliant metallic coloring.
Gall wasp. Induces plant galls, especially on oaks. Tiny.
Velvet ant. Wingless female. Very painful sting.
Pink Bow-Tie Gall Wasp. Gall wasp. Induces plant galls, especially on oaks. Tiny.
Roger's crypt ant — tiny ant in damp soil and leaf litter. Can reproduce by thelytokous parthenogenesis (no males needed). Cosmopolitan.
California citronella ant — medium yellow-orange ant producing lemon/citronella odor when crushed. Lives underground tending root aphids. Rarely seen on surface.

fm.Behavior: Underground — tends root aphids

desc: Brick honeypot ant — brick-red honeypot ant with enormously distended replete workers. Desert. Living food storage.
Black legionary ant — army ant forming raiding columns that attack other ant colonies. Nomadic — no permanent nest. Columns can contain thousands of workers.

fm.Behavior: Army ant — raids other ant colonies in massive columns

desc: Duplicate sand wasp — large bold black-and-yellow crabronid. Hunts flies. Fast aerial predator. Nests in sandy soil.

fm.Color: Bold black and yellow; hunts flies

desc: Ashmead's digger wasp — large dark wasp hunting grasshoppers and katydids. Provisions underground burrow with paralyzed prey. Named for W.H. Ashmead.
Cousin potter wasp — potter wasp building clay pot nests. Provisions with caterpillars. Related to other Eumenes potters.

fm.Color: Dark with pale bands; clay pot nests

desc: Wine-red Amazon ant — slave-making ant that raids Formica colonies, stealing pupae to raise as workers. Cannot forage or feed itself without slave workers. Remarkable biology.

fm.Note: Slave-maker — raids other ant colonies for workers

desc: Caguatan acorn ant — tiny ant nesting in small cavities — acorns, hollow stems, rock crevices. Miniature colonies.
Round gall wasp — cynipid inducing small round galls on oak. Part of the diverse oak gall wasp community.

fm.Color: Tiny; small round galls on oak

desc: Tree cricket parasitoid — tiny eurytomid wasp parasitizing tree cricket eggs. Important natural enemy of tree crickets. Remarkable host-finding ability.
Hairless rover ant — tiny smooth ant. Nests in soil under objects. Very small. Tends root-feeding insects for honeydew underground.
Charcoal carpenter ant — large dark carpenter ant. Nests in dead wood. One of several Camponotus species in LA foothills.
Hyatt's carpenter ant — large carpenter ant of mountain habitats. Nests in dead conifer wood. Named for entomologist Hyatt.
Changing acrobat ant — small ant that curls abdomen over thorax when alarmed ('acrobat'). Nests in dead wood and under bark. Tends aphids.

fm.Color: Brown; curls abdomen over back when alarmed

desc: Wheeler's fungus-farming ant — tiny ant that cultivates fungus gardens underground. One of the few non-leafcutter fungus-farming ants. Remarkable agriculture.
Common crypt ant — tiny dark ant nesting in soil and rotting wood. Predatory on small soil invertebrates. Has sting (unusual for such tiny ant).
Half-red honeypot ant — medium bicolored ant with replete workers that store honeydew. Desert habitats. Related to M. mexicanus.
Mountain harvester ant — medium red-brown ant building mound nests in mountain grassland. Harvests seeds. Painful sting.
Prairie yellowjacket — yellowjacket nesting underground in open habitats. Social colonies of thousands. Less urban than other yellowjackets.
Mushroom gall wasp — cynipid inducing mushroom-shaped galls on oak twigs. Distinctive toadstool form. Common on coast live oak.
Silver-haired thread-waisted wasp — large sphecid with silvery thoracic hair. Hunts cutworm caterpillars. Important biocontrol agent of cutworms.

fm.Color: Dark with silvery hair; hunts cutworms

desc: Big-bellied bethylid — tiny parasitoid wasp attacking wood-boring beetle larvae. Can deliver painful sting relative to size. Important natural enemy of borers.
Red-bellied mason wasp — distinctive vespid with reddish abdomen. Provisions cavity nest with weevils. Uncommon.
American yellowjacket — common yellowjacket nesting underground. Social. Can be aggressive defending nest. Important predator of garden pests. Also scavenges.
Madeira sawfly — small sawfly. On various plants. Part of the sawfly fauna.
Birch leafminer sawfly — tiny sawfly mining birch leaves. Introduced from Europe. Larvae create blotch mines. Can disfigure ornamental birches.
Fuzzy-fingered gall wasp — cynipid inducing galls on oak. Named for fuzzy gall appendages.

fm.Color: Tiny; galls with fuzzy projections on oak

desc: Wide-banded sand wasp — medium wasp with broad pale band. Hunts ants. Nests in sandy soil.

fm.Color: Dark with wide pale band; hunts ants

desc: Leafy bud gall wasp — tiny cynipid inducing leafy bud galls on oaks. Galls resemble miniature artichokes. On coast live oak.

fm.Color: Tiny; artichoke-like bud galls on oak

desc: Schick's gall wasp — cynipid inducing galls on coast live oak. Part of the remarkably diverse California oak cynipid fauna.
California sand wasp — medium wasp hunting ants. Buries paralyzed ants in sand burrow for larvae. Specialized ant predator.
Western collared ant — medium brown ant with constricted waist. Nests in soil. Seed harvester and scavenger. Common in woodland.
Fragile carpenter ant — medium-small carpenter ant. Nests in small dead branches and stems. Less robust than other Camponotus.
Maritime carpenter ant — carpenter ant of coastal habitats. Nests in dead wood near coast. Less common than inland species.
Bradley's manic ant — medium red-brown ant of mountain habitats. Painful sting. Cold-tolerant — active at lower temperatures than most ants.
Field crazy ant — tiny fast-running ant with erratic movement. In open habitats. Very quick — runs in zigzag patterns.
Andre's acorn ant — tiny ant nesting inside acorns, hollow twigs, and bark crevices. Entire colony fits inside single acorn. Remarkable micro-nesting.

fm.Color: Brown; entire colony in an acorn

desc: Kaweah gasteruptiid — medium parasitoid wasp with elongated abdomen and clubbed antennae. Parasitizes solitary bee nests. Unusual body shape.
Pale-bordered tarantula hawk — large dark spider wasp with pale wing edges. Hunts tarantulas. Impressive size.

fm.Color: Dark blue-black; pale wing margins; hunts tarantulas

desc: Graceful ichneumon — slender parasitoid wasp. Parasitizes moth and beetle larvae. Long ovipositor.
Beautiful-winged ichneumon — medium ichneumonid parasitoid. Long ovipositor for reaching host larvae in wood. Parasitizes wood-boring beetles.

fm.Color: Dark with pale markings; long ovipositor

desc: Dorsal spider wasp — medium dark pompilid hunting spiders. Paralyzes spider and provisions burrow.
Aztec thread-waisted wasp — slender wasp hunting caterpillars. Classic 'thread-waisted' body shape. Provisions underground burrow.

fm.Color: Black and orange; extremely thin waist

desc: Crabroniform beewolf — medium wasp hunting bees. Similar body form to Crabro wasps. Provisions burrow with paralyzed bees.
Cherry slug sawfly — small sawfly whose larvae ('slugs') skeletonize cherry and pear leaves. Larvae are slimy dark slug-like. Pest of fruit trees.
Port Jackson fig wasp — tiny obligate pollinator of Port Jackson fig. Ancient mutualism — each fig species has its own specific wasp. Coevolved for millions of years.
Stoddard's harvester ant — medium dark ant harvesting seeds. Long foraging columns. Desert and scrub habitats. Important seed disperser.
Torpedo gall wasp — cynipid inducing elongated torpedo-shaped galls on oak twigs. Distinctive spindle shape.
Deeply-pitted gall wasp — cynipid inducing pitted galls on oak. Gall surface with deep pits.
Placid thread-waisted wasp — slender caterpillar-hunting wasp. Carries paralyzed caterpillars to underground nest. Elegant thin waist.

fm.Color: Dark with thin waist; carries caterpillars

desc: Edwards' ichneumon — large ichneumonid parasitizing swallowtail butterfly pupae. Important natural enemy of Papilio caterpillars. Named for H. Edwards.
Dense cuckoo wasp — tiny metallic green chrysidid. Brood parasite of solitary wasp nests. Brilliant metallic coloring.
Gleaming velvet ant — tiny metallic velvet ant. Wingless female with brilliant metallic coloring. Parasitizes small ground-nesting Hymenoptera.
Edwards' velvet ant — medium wingless female wasp with pale velvet pile. Males winged. Painful sting. Parasitizes ground-nesting bees/wasps.
Dwarf sapygid — small parasitoid wasp attacking solitary bee nests. Cleptoparasite. Part of the poorly known Sapygidae.
Conspicuous fuzzy gall wasp — cynipid inducing large fuzzy galls on oak. Galls covered in dense woolly fibers. Very conspicuous.

fm.Color: Tiny; large fuzzy galls on oak — conspicuous

desc: Indecisive horntail — large wasp-like sawfly boring into dead conifer wood. Despite fearsome appearance, does not sting. Long ovipositor drills into wood.

fm.Color: Dark; wasp-like but harmless; bores into conifers

desc: Eldorado gall wasp — cynipid from Eldorado County area. Induces galls on oaks.
Cochise mason wasp — small solitary vespid. Provisions cavity nest with caterpillars. Desert edge species.
Many-banded bethylid — tiny parasitoid wasp attacking beetle larvae. Flattens body to enter beetle tunnels in wood.
Ashmead's fairyfly — one of the smallest known insects. Mymarid wasp parasitizing insect eggs. Nearly microscopic. Important biocontrol agent.
Arizona eulophid — tiny parasitoid wasp. Attacks other gall-making insects. Part of the complex community of oak gall parasitoids.
Spotted-belly mason wasp — small solitary vespid. Provisions cavity nest with caterpillars. Pale abdominal spots.
Lacewing parasitoid — metallic parasitoid wasp attacking green lacewing larvae. Hyperparasitoid. Brilliant metallic green-blue body.
Engelmann's gall wasp — cynipid inducing tiny galls on oak leaves. Part of the diverse Neuroterus genus of leaf gallers.
